content:
# Joshua Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 233-235 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_010_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Sun Stands Still — Forty-three verses of warfare at cosmic scale. Five Amorite kings unite against Gibeon for daring to make peace with Israel. Joshua marches all night from Gilgal, and YHWH fights with hailstones from heaven and a sun that refuses to set. The chapter then sweeps through the entire southern campaign — city after city falling under the ban. This is the most extraordinary battle narrative in the Hebrew Bible: heaven itself joins the fight. PNEUMA renders the poetry of v. 12-13 as poetry, the battle reports with their relentless rhythm, and lets the raw power of the text speak.]*

---

**1** When Adoni-Zedek king of Jerusalem heard that Joshua had captured Ai and put it under the ban — that he had done to Ai and its king what he had done to Jericho and its king — and that the people of Gibeon had made peace with Israel and were living among them, **2** the fear was enormous, because Gibeon was a great city, like one of the royal cities — greater than Ai — and all its men were warriors. **3** So Adoni-Zedek king of Jerusalem sent word to Hoham king of Hebron, to Piram king of Jarmuth, to Japhia king of Lachish, and to Debir king of Eglon: **4** "Come up and help me. Let us strike Gibeon, because it has made peace with Joshua and with the Sons of Israel."

**5** So the five Amorite kings joined forces — the king of Jerusalem, the king of Hebron, the king of Jarmuth, the king of Lachish, and the king of Eglon — they and all their armies. They marched up and took positions against Gibeon and attacked it.

**6** The men of Gibeon sent an urgent message to Joshua at the camp in Gilgal: "Do not abandon your servants! Come up to us quickly — rescue us! Help us! All the Amorite kings from the hill country have united against us."

**7** So Joshua marched up from Gilgal — he and his entire fighting force, all his best warriors. **8** And YHWH said to Joshua, "Do not be afraid of them. I have handed them over to you. Not one of them will stand against you."

**9** Joshua came upon them suddenly, having marched all night from Gilgal. **10** And YHWH threw them into panic before Israel. He struck them with a great slaughter at Gibeon and chased them along the road that goes up to Beth-Horon, cutting them down all the way to Azekah and Makkedah. **11** As they fled before Israel on the descent from Beth-Horon, YHWH hurled enormous hailstones down on them from the sky, all the way to Azekah, and they died. More died from the hailstones than the Sons of Israel killed with the sword.

**12** On the day YHWH gave the Amorites over to the Sons of Israel, Joshua spoke to YHWH, and he said in the sight of Israel:

> "Sun — stand still over Gibeon!
> Moon — stop over the Valley of Aijalon!"

**13** And the sun stood still,
and the moon stopped,
until the nation took vengeance on its enemies.

Is this not written in the Book of Jashar? The sun stopped in the middle of the sky and was in no hurry to set for about a full day. **14** There has never been a day like it, before or since — a day when YHWH listened to the voice of a man. For YHWH was fighting for Israel.

**15** Then Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.

**16** Now the five kings had fled and hidden themselves in the cave at Makkedah. **17** It was reported to Joshua: "The five kings have been found, hiding in the cave at Makkedah." **18** Joshua said, "Roll large stones against the mouth of the cave and post men there to guard it. **19** But you — do not stop! Chase your enemies and cut down their rear guard. Do not let them reach their cities, because YHWH your God has given them into your hand."

**20** When Joshua and the Sons of Israel had finished striking them with a very great slaughter until they were wiped out — though some survivors escaped into the fortified cities — **21** the entire army returned safely to Joshua at the camp in Makkedah. No one dared to say a word against the Sons of Israel.

**22** Then Joshua said, "Open the mouth of the cave and bring those five kings out to me." **23** They did so, and brought the five kings out of the cave to him — the king of Jerusalem, the king of Hebron, the king of Jarmuth, the king of Lachish, and the king of Eglon. **24** When they had brought these kings to Joshua, Joshua summoned every man of Israel and said to the commanders of the warriors who had marched with him, "Come forward. Put your feet on the necks of these kings." So they came forward and put their feet on their necks. **25** Joshua said to them, "Do not be afraid, and do not lose heart. Be strong and courageous, because this is what YHWH will do to all the enemies you fight." **26** After this, Joshua struck them down and killed them, and hung their bodies on five trees. They remained hanging on the trees until evening. **27** At sunset, Joshua gave the order, and they took them down from the trees and threw them into the cave where they had hidden. They placed large stones against the mouth of the cave — and they are there to this day.

**28** That same day Joshua captured Makkedah and struck it with the sword, along with its king. He put it under the ban — every living thing in it. He left no survivor. He did to the king of Makkedah what he had done to the king of Jericho.

**29** Then Joshua moved on from Makkedah, and all Israel with him, to Libnah, and fought against Libnah. **30** YHWH gave it too, along with its king, into Israel's hand. He struck it with the sword — every living thing in it. He left no survivor. He did to its king what he had done to the king of Jericho.

**31** Then Joshua moved on from Libnah, and all Israel with him, to Lachish. He laid siege to it and attacked it. **32** YHWH gave Lachish into Israel's hand. He captured it on the second day and struck it with the sword — every living thing in it — just as he had done to Libnah.

**33** Then Horam king of Gezer came up to help Lachish, but Joshua struck him down along with his people until no survivor remained.

**34** Then Joshua moved on from Lachish, and all Israel with him, to Eglon. They laid siege to it and attacked it. **35** They captured it that same day and struck it with the sword. He put every living thing in it under the ban that day — just as he had done to Lachish.

**36** Then Joshua went up from Eglon, and all Israel with him, to Hebron, and they attacked it. **37** They captured it and struck it with the sword — its king, all its towns, and every living thing in it. He left no survivor — just as he had done to Eglon. He put it and every living thing in it under the ban.

**38** Then Joshua turned, and all Israel with him, to Debir, and attacked it. **39** He captured it along with its king and all its towns. They struck them with the sword and put every living thing in it under the ban. He left no survivor. What he had done to Hebron, and what he had done to Libnah and its king, he did to Debir and its king.

**40** So Joshua struck the whole land — the hill country, the Negev, the foothills, and the mountain slopes — along with all their kings. He left no survivor. Everything that breathed he put under the ban, just as YHWH the God of Israel had commanded. **41** Joshua struck them from Kadesh-Barnea to Gaza, and all the land of Goshen, as far as Gibeon. **42** All these kings and their land Joshua captured in a single campaign, because YHWH the God of Israel fought for Israel.

**43** Then Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1 — "Adoni-Zedek" ('adoni-tsedeq):** The name means "my lord is righteousness" — an ironic title for a king who is about to lose everything. Compare Melchizedek ("king of righteousness"), the earlier king of Salem/Jerusalem in Genesis 14. The Canaanite throne of Jerusalem carries theophoric names of justice, yet here stands opposed to YHWH's purpose. PNEUMA transliterates without comment in the text and lets the name speak.

**V. 1 — "Put it under the ban" (hekherim):** The Hebrew root kh-r-m (cherem) is the vocabulary of total dedication to destruction — consecration by annihilation. PNEUMA uses "put under the ban" throughout this chapter as a consistent rendering. It is not casual destruction; it is ritual, vowed, and absolute. The word carries the weight of sacred obligation, however disturbing to modern ears.

**V. 2 — "The fear was enormous" (vayyir'u me'od):** Literally "they feared greatly." The subject is ambiguous in Hebrew — it could be Adoni-Zedek alone or the collective reaction. PNEUMA renders impersonally ("the fear was enormous") to capture the atmosphere: Gibeon's defection to Israel terrified the entire region.

**V. 6 — "Do not abandon your servants" ('al tereph yadekha me'avadekha):** Literally "do not let your hands drop from your servants" — a vivid idiom meaning "do not let go of us, do not slacken your grip." The Gibeonites invoke the treaty. They are now Israel's dependents and call themselves "your servants." PNEUMA renders the meaning directly: "Do not abandon your servants."

**V. 10 — "YHWH threw them into panic" (vayyehumem YHWH):** The verb h-m-m means to confuse, rout, throw into panic — the same verb used of YHWH's action at the Red Sea (Exodus 14:24) and against the Philistines (1 Samuel 7:10). This is not merely a military advantage; it is divine terror. YHWH fights by dismantling the enemy's capacity to think.

**V. 11 — "Enormous hailstones" ('avanim gedoloth):** Literally "great stones." The text specifies these are "stones of hail" ('avne barad). The scale is deliberate: more died from heaven's bombardment than from Israel's swords. The narrator wants the reader to understand who actually won this battle. PNEUMA renders "enormous hailstones" to capture the supernatural scale.

**V. 12-13 — The poem of the sun standing still:** These verses shift from narrative prose to poetry — a quotation from the lost Book of Jashar (Sefer ha-Yashar, also cited in 2 Samuel 1:18). The Hebrew uses two imperatives: dom (be still/be silent) for the sun, and 'amod (stand/stop) for the moon. PNEUMA renders this as poetry with line breaks and the direct commanding voice: "Sun — stand still! Moon — stop!" The Hebrew dom carries overtones of silence and cessation, not merely of motion but of activity. The sun is commanded to be quiet, to cease its work of moving.

**V. 13 — "Was in no hurry to set" (lo 'atz lavo'):** Literally "did not hasten to go" — a remarkable anthropomorphism. The sun is depicted as a being that could hurry or delay. PNEUMA renders "was in no hurry to set" — preserving the personification. The sun is patient, unhurried, lingering so that Israel can finish the fight.

**V. 13 — "The Book of Jashar" (Sefer ha-Yashar):** A lost collection of ancient Israelite poetry, cited here and in 2 Samuel 1:18 (David's lament for Saul and Jonathan). The name means "Book of the Upright" or "Book of the Straight." Its citation proves the narrator is drawing on older written sources. PNEUMA transliterates "Jashar" rather than translating, following convention.

**V. 14 — "YHWH listened to the voice of a man":** This is the narrator's astonished commentary. The extraordinary thing is not the cosmic miracle but the relational reality behind it: God listened to a human being. The Hebrew shama' b'qol — "listened to the voice of" — is the language of obedience (the same phrase used for obeying God). Here, astonishingly, the direction is reversed. PNEUMA preserves the plain statement and lets its strangeness land.

**V. 21 — "No one dared to say a word against" (lo charats... l'ish leshono):** Literally "no one sharpened his tongue against any man of the Sons of Israel." The idiom means no one dared to speak a hostile word — the enemy was so thoroughly broken that even verbal opposition ceased. PNEUMA renders the meaning: "No one dared to say a word against."

**V. 24 — "Put your feet on the necks of these kings":** This is not gratuitous humiliation but the ancient Near Eastern ritual of dominion. The victor places his foot on the neck of the conquered — a symbolic act depicted in Egyptian and Mesopotamian art. Joshua uses it as a teaching moment (v. 25): what YHWH has done to these kings, he will do to all your enemies. The act is pedagogical, not merely punitive.

**V. 27 — "They are there to this day":** The etiological formula ('ad ha-yom ha-zeh) — the narrator writes from a later vantage point, when the cave with its stones was still a known landmark. This is the voice of the historian explaining how a place got its story.

**V. 28-39 — The southern campaign litany:** Verses 28-39 follow a rigid literary formula: Joshua moves to a city, attacks it, YHWH gives it into his hand, he strikes it with the sword, puts it under the ban, leaves no survivor, and the account closes with a comparison to a previous conquest. PNEUMA preserves this repetitive structure deliberately. The drumbeat repetition is the point — it conveys the relentless, unstoppable sweep of the campaign. Each city falls with the same inevitability.

**V. 40 — "Everything that breathed" (kol ha-neshamah):** The noun neshamah is the breath of life — the same word used in Genesis 2:7 when God breathes life into the first human. Here it is inverted: every bearer of that divine breath is destroyed. PNEUMA renders literally. The theological tension is left unresolved, as the text leaves it.

**V. 42 — "In a single campaign":** The Hebrew phrase b'pa'am 'akhath means "at one time" or "in one go." PNEUMA renders "in a single campaign" to convey the temporal compression: the entire southern conquest is presented as one continuous military operation, not a series of separate wars.

**On the Vaticanus LXX:** The Greek generally follows the Hebrew in this chapter, though with some notable differences. The LXX of v. 15 is absent in some manuscripts (Vaticanus omits it), suggesting it may be a later harmonization with v. 43. The five-king narrative and the southern campaign list are preserved in the same order. The poetic section (vv. 12-13) is rendered in prose in the Greek, losing the Hebrew's shift in register. PNEUMA follows the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex as primary source.

— PNEUMA
